text: I’ll inspect the fixture dependency manifests and test setup first, then run the audit tool against the actual app environment so the fixes are grounded in the repo rather than guesses.

The repo root only has the provided AGENTS instructions. I found a Python service under `fixtures/service` with pinned requirements, so I’m narrowing the audit to that manifest and the root `make test` flow.

Several pins are already deliberately old, so I’m using `pip-audit`’s fix/version data to distinguish real remediations from packages that only look stale. Next I’ll audit both the pinned requirements and the installed test environment if needed.

The first audit reports 51 advisories across 11 packages. The JSON output is large, so I’m going to capture a compact column view and then test candidate upgrades against the pinned dependency graph before editing.

The actionable package set is `urllib3`, `cryptography`, `flask`, `idna`, `mako`, `pillow`, `pydantic`, `pymysql`, `python-dotenv`, `requests`, and `werkzeug`. I’m checking whether the highest required fixed versions actually resolve with the app’s older Flask/extension stack before changing files.

text: The first candidate set hit a real resolver conflict: `cryptography==48.0.1` requires `cffi>=2.0.0`, while the lock pins `cffi==1.17.1`. Since `cffi` is a transitive requirement already in the lock, I’m testing the smallest related lock update needed for the security fix.

text: The audit is now down from 51 advisories across 11 packages to the 4 documented `cryptography` advisories that can’t be installed around `pybluemonday`’s `cffi` constraint. I’m running the project’s required `make test` next; if compatibility issues appear, I’ll keep the fixes scoped to what the dependency upgrades require.

`make test` now reaches collection but fails because `Flask-Babel==2.0.0` imports a Flask helper removed in Flask 3. That package must move with Flask; I’m checking current compatible extension versions and will keep the changes to these upgrade-induced breaks.
`Flask-Babel` has a Flask 3-compatible `4.0.0` release. I’m bumping that input pin and regenerating the lock, then I’ll rerun the required tests.
That compile command was pointed at the repo root instead of `fixtures/service`, so it didn’t change anything. I’m rerunning it from the fixture directory.
`Flask-Babel` is now compatible and pulled newer `Babel`/`pytz` transitively. I’m rerunning `make test` from the root to find the next actual compatibility break, if any.

The next failure is from a new transitive dependency (`referencing`) importing the modern `attrs` module name, while the old lock kept `attrs==20.3.0`. I’m updating that transitive pin to match the newer RESTX stack.
The `attrs` lock update succeeded. I accidentally invoked the fixture’s own Makefile, which expects a global `pytest`; I’m rerunning the required root `make test` now.

Tests now fail in one app compatibility point: Flask 3 removed `app.session_cookie_name`. I’m patching the custom session interface to read `SESSION_COOKIE_NAME` from config, which is the Flask-supported replacement.

I found three uses in the custom session interface. I’m replacing them with one local `cookie_name` value from `app.config["SESSION_COOKIE_NAME"]` so behavior stays the same and the Flask 3 API is respected.
The Flask 3 session-cookie compatibility patch is in place. I’m rerunning the root test target to continue through any remaining upgrade-related breaks.

Werkzeug 3 no longer accepts a bytes cookie value. The app’s signer returns bytes, so I’m decoding the signed session ID before `set_cookie`; this preserves the wire value while matching the newer API.
The signed cookie value is now text before it reaches Werkzeug. I’m rerunning the test target to see whether the dependency upgrade has any further app-facing incompatibilities.

The suite is now running substantially further: most tests are passing, with a smaller set of failures still executing. I’m waiting for the full failure summary before making the next targeted change.

Only 9 tests are failing now, all around redirect `Location` headers being relative under newer Flask/Werkzeug where the old tests expect absolute `http://localhost/...`. I’m locating the app/test-client hook that can restore the previous absolute-location behavior without changing individual routes.

Werkzeug’s response behavior changed to leave relative redirects alone. I’m checking whether its `Response.autocorrect_location_header` compatibility flag is still present; if it is, setting the app response class is the narrowest fix.

tool:
/bin/bash -lc "fixtures/service/.venv/bin/python - <<'PY'
from werkzeug.wrappers import Response
print(hasattr(Response, 'autocorrect_location_header'))
print(getattr(Response, 'autocorrect_location_header', None))
from flask import Response as FlaskResponse
print(hasattr(FlaskResponse, 'autocorrect_location_header'))
print(getattr(FlaskResponse, 'autocorrect_location_header', None))
PY"

text: The compatibility flag exists and now defaults to `False`. I’m adding a CTFd response subclass with `autocorrect_location_header = True` and wiring it into the app class to restore the old absolute redirect locations.
The response class now restores absolute redirect locations for this app. I’m rerunning `make test`; at this point I expect either a clean pass or only very specific remaining compatibility failures.
The rerun is still in progress and only one failure has appeared so far in the progress line. I’m waiting for the final summary before touching anything else.

The last failure is a query encoding difference: Flask 3 leaves `/` unescaped inside `next=...`, while this app/test contract expects `%2F`. I’m patching the login redirect helper path to encode the `next` value explicitly.

This exact assertion comes from the `authed_only` redirect path. I’m changing that path to build the `next` query with `urllib.parse.quote`, matching the old encoded Location value without touching unrelated redirects.
The remaining redirect path now percent-encodes `next` explicitly. I’m running the required test target again.
The progress output still shows one failure marker, so I’m waiting for the summary to see if it is the same assertion or a different redirect path.
The failing `/challenges` redirect comes from `check_challenge_visibility`, not the general auth decorator. I’m applying the same explicit `next` encoding in that specific visibility path.
The challenge-visibility redirect now matches the old encoded `next` format too. I’m rerunning `make test` again.
I patched the score-visibility branch first; `/challenges` uses the challenge-visibility branch below it. I found the remaining unencoded redirect and will patch that after this run finishes.
The rerun confirms the same remaining branch. I’m patching the `ChallengeVisibilityTypes.PRIVATE` redirect now.
Both challenge visibility login redirects now explicitly encode `next`. I’m running the root test target again.
The current test run has passed the previous failure area and is still progressing cleanly. I’m waiting for the final result before documenting the fixes.
`make test` now passes: 82 passed. I’m running the final audit and then I’ll write `FIXES.md` with one entry per vulnerable package, including the unresolved `cryptography` constraint.

The final audit confirms the only remaining advisories are `cryptography` ones blocked by the `pybluemonday`/`cffi` constraint. I’m adding `FIXES.md` now with one package-level entry for each vulnerable package found in the original audit.
